caption: Fig. 2 Multiphase microstructure evolution during rolling and heat treatment.
Fig. 3 Evolution of RA during thermomechanical processing.
Fig. 4 Identification of B and TM based on TEM and discovery of RA.
citation_sentences: Bainite is a microstructure composed of bainitic ferrite and carbides, with the carbides inside the bainitic ferrite distributed at a 60°?? angle to its boundaries. In tempered martensite, however, carbides are randomly distributed.32 This difference in orientation helps distinguish bainite from tempered martensite, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Despite these differences, EBSD struggles to distinguish the two structures due to their similar mechanical properties. Thus, they are grouped as B/TM.33 The content of B/TM and FM is quantified using the Image Quality (IQ) values of Kikuchi patterns from EBSD. Fig. 5(a) presents the combined Kikuchi pattern quality and phase distribution maps for AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, obtained from EBSD. Differences in carbon concentration and dislocation density cause the band contrast of martensitic phases to be lower than that of ferrite, bainite, and retained austenite.34 Several points within the white dashed boxes were sampled and averaged to determine the critical IQ values for fresh martensite, bainite/tempered martensite, and ferrite. As shown in Fig. 5(b), all three samples exhibit a distinct bimodal distribution of IQ values for the BCC structure. This pattern suggests that bainite/tempered martensite and fresh martensite, with an average size of about 1 lm, along with ferrite, averaging about 5 lm, make up a substantial portion of the volume. The decrease in retained austenite content is primarily driven by reduced austenite stability. Retained austenite contents for AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T are 7.4%, 2.4%, and 0.6%, respectively.

caption: Fig. 5 Phases separation strategy and interphase strain analysis based on EBSD.
citation_sentences: Bainite is a microstructure composed of bainitic ferrite and carbides, with the carbides inside the bainitic ferrite distributed at a 60°?? angle to its boundaries. In tempered martensite, however, carbides are randomly distributed.32 This difference in orientation helps distinguish bainite from tempered martensite, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Despite these differences, EBSD struggles to distinguish the two structures due to their similar mechanical properties. Thus, they are grouped as B/TM.33 The content of B/TM and FM is quantified using the Image Quality (IQ) values of Kikuchi patterns from EBSD. Fig. 5(a) presents the combined Kikuchi pattern quality and phase distribution maps for AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, obtained from EBSD. Differences in carbon concentration and dislocation density cause the band contrast of martensitic phases to be lower than that of ferrite, bainite, and retained austenite.34 Several points within the white dashed boxes were sampled and averaged to determine the critical IQ values for fresh martensite, bainite/tempered martensite, and ferrite. As shown in Fig. 5(b), all three samples exhibit a distinct bimodal distribution of IQ values for the BCC structure. This pattern suggests that bainite/tempered martensite and fresh martensite, with an average size of about 1 lm, along with ferrite, averaging about 5 lm, make up a substantial portion of the volume. The decrease in retained austenite content is primarily driven by reduced austenite stability. Retained austenite contents for AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T are 7.4%, 2.4%, and 0.6%, respectively.

According to the analysis of Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) (Fig. 5(c)), it is found that there are significant strain concentrations at the boundaries between ferrite and bainite/martensite. Fig. 5(d) shows the point-to-origin analysis of the magnified areas within the black boxes in Fig. 5(c), the KAM value reaches its peak when the characteristic line crosses the boundary and then gradually decreases. This is attributed to the dislocations accumulated at grain boundaries, coordinating phases with different mechanical responses during deformation to maintain the continuity of deformation.35,36 The analysis selected multiple sets of characteristic lines for each sample and calculated their average values to reflect the objective trend. In Fig. 5(d), only 2–3 representative positions are marked. AN800-T has the highest average KAM $( \mathrm { K A M _ { A V E } } = 0 . 6 9 ^ { \circ } )$ , due to its higher content of bainite and martensite. The content and size of phases were statistically analyzed as depicted in Figs. 6(a) and (b). As the annealing temperature increases, the ferrite content rises, while the sizes of bainite and martensite decrease. RA can transform into martensite during deformation through the TRIP effect, enhancing the strength and ductility of materials. Consequently, the low-density steel develops a multiphase microstructure composed of F, B, TM, RA, and FM.

EBSD characterization was conducted on the necking region, compared to the state before tensile deformation, the RA content in AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T decreased (Fig. 5(a)). The yellow dashed box areas in Fig. 12(a) were selected for magnified characterization. Stress concentration mainly occurs in the hard regions represented by B/TM and FM (highlighted in red). The white pixel areas are regions prone to micropore nucleation and expansion during deformation (Fig. 12(b)). In Fig. 12(a), characteristic lines (yellow arrows) were selected for Point-to-Point (P-T-P) orientation difference and Kikuchi pattern quality statistics (Fig. 12(c)), where $\mathrm { P - T - P _ { A v e } }$ is the average of P-T-P orientation. When the characteristic line crosses the phase boundary, the orientation difference increases sharply, and the Kikuchi pattern quality drops to a minimum, indicating significant strain and intense dislocation accumulation at the phase interface. The stress distribution behavior between the soft domain and hard domain changes significantly to accommodate the discontinuity of strain.83,84

citation_sentences: When polycrystalline materials undergo deformation, the continuity restricts the deformation of adjacent grains, and the higher total grain boundary area provides greater deformation resistance, thereby enhancing the strength of materials. The average size of each phase has been counted in Fig. 6(b). The Hall-Petch formula is used to represent the effort of grain refinement to the strength of the material:60–63

caption: Fig. 8 Quantitative strategy of phases composition.
citation_sentences: When analyzing the mass fraction of solid solution elements, observations were made using EPMA, carbon elements are mainly concentrated in B/TM, FM, and RA (Fig. 8(a)). The data selected along the red dashed lines are shown in Fig. 8(b), indicating that with increasing annealing temperature, the distribution of carbon elements becomes more uniform, while the distribution of other elements shows no significant differences. The average elemental compositions of phases AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T are summarized in Table A1 in Appendix A.

caption: Fig. 9 Constitutive relationship fitting curve and experimental data comparison of AN750-T, AN800-T and AN850-T.
citation_sentences: After the analysis of Section 4.1.1, the parameters obtained from the experiments were input into the constitutive relationship, as depicted in Fig. 9. Overall, as the true strain increases, the difference between the constitutive relationship established by the model and the mechanical performance curve obtained experimentally gradually increases. The TEM microstructure in Fig. 10 illustrates a gradient accumulation of GNDs at the boundaries. As the degree of strain localization increases, the strain gradient also increases, resulting in back stress $\left( { \boldsymbol { \sigma } } _ { \mathbf { B } } \right)$ work hardening, as illustrated in the shaded area of Fig. 9. When AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T reached the necking strain, the maximum stress caused by $\sigma _ { \mathrm { B } } = 2 1 7 , 3 0 0 , 2 7 8 \mathrm { M P a } .$ , respectively. Combined with continuous DIC strain fields, strain concentration, distribution, and re-concentration were observed. The inset shows the DIC strain fields, the AN750- T sample exhibits relatively uniform strain during the yield stage, and the phenomenon of stress distribution is observed during the uniform plastic deformation stage (as indicated by the circle in Fig. 9(a)). In the AN800-T sample, two local strain concentration points are observed. As the strain progresses, these points expand at a $4 5 ^ { \circ }$ angle and connect with each other, leading to necking in the material. This phenomenon was also found in the works of Liu et al.76,77 The hard phase would bear a relatively large stress to ‘accelerate’ the increase of its strain, while the soft domain would bear a relatively smaller load to ‘slow down’ the increase of its strain, thereby ensuring the continuity of strain between the hard and soft domain in the microstructure and achieving the purpose of plasticity enhancement. The strain distribution phenomenon is more pronounced in AN750-T, which is closely related to the strain-hardening behavior induced by TRIP during the deformation process. However, this phenomenon was also observed in AN800-T and AN850-T with lower RA content, further confirming that the coordinated strain between the soft domain and hard domain effectively prevents premature failure.

caption: Fig. 10 TEM analysis of microstructure of soft domain and hard domain.
citation_sentences: After the analysis of Section 4.1.1, the parameters obtained from the experiments were input into the constitutive relationship, as depicted in Fig. 9. Overall, as the true strain increases, the difference between the constitutive relationship established by the model and the mechanical performance curve obtained experimentally gradually increases. The TEM microstructure in Fig. 10 illustrates a gradient accumulation of GNDs at the boundaries. As the degree of strain localization increases, the strain gradient also increases, resulting in back stress $\left( { \boldsymbol { \sigma } } _ { \mathbf { B } } \right)$ work hardening, as illustrated in the shaded area of Fig. 9. When AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T reached the necking strain, the maximum stress caused by $\sigma _ { \mathrm { B } } = 2 1 7 , 3 0 0 , 2 7 8 \mathrm { M P a } .$ , respectively. Combined with continuous DIC strain fields, strain concentration, distribution, and re-concentration were observed. The inset shows the DIC strain fields, the AN750- T sample exhibits relatively uniform strain during the yield stage, and the phenomenon of stress distribution is observed during the uniform plastic deformation stage (as indicated by the circle in Fig. 9(a)). In the AN800-T sample, two local strain concentration points are observed. As the strain progresses, these points expand at a $4 5 ^ { \circ }$ angle and connect with each other, leading to necking in the material. This phenomenon was also found in the works of Liu et al.76,77 The hard phase would bear a relatively large stress to ‘accelerate’ the increase of its strain, while the soft domain would bear a relatively smaller load to ‘slow down’ the increase of its strain, thereby ensuring the continuity of strain between the hard and soft domain in the microstructure and achieving the purpose of plasticity enhancement. The strain distribution phenomenon is more pronounced in AN750-T, which is closely related to the strain-hardening behavior induced by TRIP during the deformation process. However, this phenomenon was also observed in AN800-T and AN850-T with lower RA content, further confirming that the coordinated strain between the soft domain and hard domain effectively prevents premature failure.

where m is the Poisson’s coefficient, $\nu = 0 . 2 8 ; \Delta \rho _ { \mathrm { G N D } }$ is the dif-GND ference in GND density between adjacent regions34; R is the average characteristic size of the back stress influence zone; Dh is the average thickness of the unit heterogeneous layer, obtained by averaging several TEM structures using the method shown in Fig. 10(a). By combining Eq. (16) for calculation, the evolution of additional strain hardening of GNDs with strain can be obtained.

Fig. 10(a) shows the TEM microstructure of AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T before deformation, with F surrounded by hard domains. The mechanical incompatibility generated by phases during deformation, a strain gradient forms at the interface between the soft domain and hard domain, leading to the release of mass GNDs at the phase boundaries. Accompanying these GNDs is the back stress generated by them in the direction opposite to the applied stress, as depicted in Fig. 10(c). The internal stress generated in the F is compressive stress, thereby weakening the promoting effect of external tensile loads on dislocation motion within ferrite grains, making dislocation slip relatively difficult compared to the original situation, and slowing down the rate of increase in grain plastic strain. At the same time, the internal stresses generated in the adjacent B/TM and FM to the F are in the same direction as the tensile direction. In other words, the coordinated deformation behavior leads to the soft domain hardening and the hard domain softening, thus maintaining the continuity of deformation between phases as much as possible, and avoiding premature initiation of micro-porosity due to deformation incompatibility.27

The model calculation results are shown in Fig. 10(e). The model fits well with the experimental data, but at the initial stage of strain, the calculated values of the model are higher. This is partly because some non-strain functions, such as solid solution strengthening, boundary strengthening, and Peierls stress, were considered in the model. Additionally, Pierce et al. explained, suggesting that the nonuniform distribution of carbon in FM would lead to a mixed microstructure with different hardness regions.79 These regions would yield successively under applied stress, resulting in a continuous stress– strain curve at low-stress levels. Compared to AN800-T and AN850-T, the relatively lower concentration of carbon elements in FM in AN750-T leads to a smaller discrepancy between model calculations and experimental values at the initial stages of strain. The strength contribution of B/TM is higher than other phases, and with increasing annealing temperature, reaching 23%, 41%, and 54% of the flow stress at necking strain. The relationship is not solely tied to the B/ TM content but is significantly influenced by carbon concentration. The content of RA decreases with increasing strain, yet the stress carried by the remaining RA shows no significant decrease. During the strain process, the RA transforms into DIM, which has a smaller average free path for dislocations, leading to a significantly increased rate of dislocation accumulation.80 Therefore, the stress–strain curves of DIM exhibit higher strain hardening values than B/TM. For AN850-T, the lower content of RA results in no significant strainhardening phenomenon induced by DIM. Furthermore, under necking strain, the back stress strengthening contributions caused by GND in AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T are 236, 291, 254 MPa, respectively, which are closely related to the synergistic deformation mechanism of the multiphase heterogeneous structure.

caption: Fig. 11 Crack path and dimple statistics of multiphase steels were characterized by SEM.
citation_sentences: Microscale strain localization can lead to the initiation of microcracks, while the plastic anisotropy of different microstructures can significantly alter the propagation path of cracks.81,82 The necking region after tensile fracture (indicated by the red dot in Fig. 11) was selected for SEM analysis to gather key insights into the fracture behavior. Microscale voids (yellow dashed boxes) are mainly distributed at the interface of the soft domain and hard domain. If the deformation is severe enough, these voids will coalesce, leading to microvoid coalescence fracture, as depicted in Fig. 11(a). In AN750-T and AN800-T, the crack tip blunts at the hard domain interface, generating secondary cracks (yellow arrows). The driving force for crack propagation is lower than the resistance provided by the hard domains, allowing cracks to extend into other easily nucleated areas. In AN850-T, the hard domains were observed to be severed (red arrows). At 850 °??C, significant migration of carbon and manganese into the B/TM and FM regions results in local oversaturation of alloying elements, increasing the strength but also the brittleness of the hard domains. The fracture morphology shows large dimples surrounded by smaller ones (Fig. 11(b)). AN750-T displays relatively deep dimples, whereas AN800-T and AN850-T exhibit some quasi-cleavage fractures, mostly originating from fresh martensite. Dimple size distribution was analyzed using Nano Measure software (Fig. 11(c)), revealing a bimodal distribution, with AN750-T having larger average dimple sizes. This suggests that the coarse ferrite and fine hard phases effectively alleviate stress concentration during deformation.
